text: We present a case of laparoscopic repair of paracetamide-induced vesicle vagina fistula in metastatic colon cancer.
A fifty-four years old female had a second adenocarcinoma with peritoneal seeding over the cul-de-sac and uterus.
The surgeon performs a right hemicolectomy, excises seeding tumors, and conducts a hysterectomy along with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, removing the uterus and ovaries.
The final pathology was stage IVB, so we started Bevacizumab Plus for theory biweekly.
However, the patient complained of urine from the vagina after four courses of the treatment.
The laparoscopic view reveals a vesicovaginal fistula approximately three millimeters in length, clearly visible through the use of scissors for dissection.
The colposcope reveals a fistula opening on the left aspect of the vaginal stump, measuring approximately eight millimeters in size.
Urine is visibly leaking from the fistula opening on the left aspect of the vaginal stump.
After full course of the treatment, abdominal CT scan shows stable disease without tumor recurrence.
So we decided to do laparoscopic repair of the fistula after discontinuation of Bevacizumab for three months.
Before the surgery, we performed the cystoscope. A guide wire was inserted from the vagina stump and advanced into the bladder.
The cystoscope view reveals the fistula opening located at the trigone near the left ureteral orifice, providing a clear visualization of the anatomical position of the fistula.
The surgeon begins the dissection of the bladder base from the vaginal stump, with the guide wire visibly positioned at the dissection site.
due to adhesion between bladder and vagina stump.
The surgeon creates a cystotomy on the left aspect of the bladder, revealing the fistula opening within the bladder cavity.
The surgeon dissects toward the posterior wall of the bladder, exposing the fistula tract outside the bladder.
The surgeon extends the cystotomy edge to further expose the vaginal opening of the fistula, revealing the anatomical connection between the bladder and vagina.
The surgeon has fully exposed the vaginal opening, revealing its anatomical location and surrounding tissue.
The surgeon removes fibrotic tissue and trims the edge of the vagina opening. The opening is then closed using Vigrio absorbable sutures.
The surgeon closes the cystotomy wound on the bladder with a 3-0 Monocryl absorbable suture, completing the repair.
After the surgery, the patient recovered well without complaint of urine from the vagina. One month later, the cystography showed no recurrence of the vesicle vagina fistula.
